metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
metadata.notes: Core/restored mushroom records retained at the original mushrooms.json path. Expanded mushroom records now load from smaller grouped files listed in split_manifest. v4.2.80 adds a core mushroom credibility pass: source links, use roles, foraging values, card notes, and cleaned placeholder use text for high-visibility mushrooms. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.notes: Old Man of the Woods is kept under the common-name slug. The former scientific-name slug strobilomyces-strobilaceus has been merged here to avoid duplicate listings.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice.
records.edibility_detail: Not a prime edible. Use only young, firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor-textured and unappealing.
records.field_identification: Distinctive gray-black woolly/scaly bolete with a shaggy cap and stem. Pores start whitish-gray and darken; cut flesh turns pinkish or red, then blackens over time.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Old Man of the Woods
use_links.notes: Scaly cap, pore color, hardwood association, and staining progression.

pore_color: Whitish to gray pores becoming blackish
cap_surface: Woolly black scales over gray-white base
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often July through October.
mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Old Man of the Woods. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_notes: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice. Not a prime edible.

mushroom_profile.summary: A lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically processed as famine food or flour extender, but bitter acids require substantial preparation.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round in open, well-drained sites.
research_notes: Better treated as a lichen entry than a mushroom entry.
Known more as animal browse and famine food than as a casual wild edible.

mushroom_profile.summary: Actually a rock-dwelling lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically used as an emergency or famine food after extensive processing.
research_notes: Common name covers edible Umbilicaria lichens rather than a single mushroom species.
Treat as a survival food entry, not a routine edible.
Slow-growing and best not heavily harvested.

records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Leccinum scabrum to illustrate the broader scaber-stalk birch/aspen bolete group.
records.duplicate_note: Broad scaber-stalk duplicate/group page kept only as a legacy slug alias. Its caution content has been consolidated into Birch Bolete / Leccinum scabrum.

records.notes: Fast-deliquescing gilled mushroom of disturbed ground.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible only when young, white, and before the gills darken/ink. Cook soon after picking; it deteriorates quickly.
records.general_notes: This is a classic 'find it and use it quickly' species. The deliquescing black gills are part of why it must sit in the gilled lane.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in lawns, wood chips, roadsides, and disturbed ground; most common in late summer and fall, with occasional spring reports.

records.field_identification: Tall shaggy white cap that becomes black ink as it matures; often in lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible but extremely time-sensitive: use young white specimens before they ink.
image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo batch-pack candidate appended in v4.2.61-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack1 (Batch B — common edible forms).

records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Edible reports exist, but quality is variable and ID can be confusing; better treated as a cautious/advanced edible unless converted into lobster mushroom.
records.general_notes: A good reminder that russulas belong in the gilled lane too. The short thick stem and brittle white flesh matter more than generic 'mushroom' features.
reviewReasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative
review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative
records.review_note: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative.
records.review_notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative.
records.field_identification: Sturdy pale russula with short thick stem; gills and stem often bruise brownish. Can be hard to separate from similar Lactarius without checking for latex.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair/advanced russula; useful partly because it can become lobster mushroom.

records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage. Russula group with seafood-like odor.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Often considered edible when confidently identified, but North American shrimp russulas are a complex; use only with strong Russula skills.
records.general_notes: This belongs in the gilled lane even though it does not look like a typical agaric. Russula brittleness and odor are important criteria for the mushroom schema.

Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative

records.review_notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Russula species/group ID should be kept conservative.
records.scientific_name: Russula xerampelina group / North American shrimp russulas
records.field_identification: Russulas with shrimp/fishy odor, brown staining, mild taste, and brittle flesh; species-level ID is messy in North America.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair edible for Russula people; shrimp odor helps, but the group is taxonomically messy.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushroom. Cook fresh in butter, eggs, sauces, or cream dishes; avoid confusing with jack-o'-lanterns.
records.field_identification: Orange-yellow chanterelle with a smooth to shallowly wrinkled underside rather than true blade-like gills.
records.mushroom_card_note: Prime chanterelle-type edible with a smooth or barely wrinkled underside.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually starts in July and can continue into early fall after wet periods in hardwoods.

records.notes: Hard hoof-shaped bracket on hardwoods, especially birch and beech. The tough gray-brown hoof form and pale pore underside are the key field marks.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional and supplement interest, especially as tea/extract; this app does not treat it as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Too tough for table eating; usually dried and simmered for tea/decoction.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Thin leathery shelf with strongly zoned, fuzzy cap surface and a true tiny-pored white underside.
records.mushroom_card_note: Tea/decoction mushroom, not table food; confirm true pores underneath.
